coredata.dc:title: High selectivity sensing of cobalt in HepG2 cells based on necklace model microenvironment-modulated carbon dot-improved chemiluminescence in Fenton-like system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Jiexuan
Lu, Chao
Yan, Dan
Ma, Lina
coredata.dc:description: In this work, based on electronic spinning resonance and chemiluminescence (CL) measurements, it is found that the reaction kinetics of Co(II)-triggered Fenton-like reaction is much faster than those of other transition metal ions, which facilitates the observation of the strong CL signals from Co(II). Utilizing the polymer–surfactant complexes (i.e., necklace model) to act as a novel CL microenvironment, a highly selective CL sensing system for the detection of Co(II) has been successfully fabricated based on the CL resonance energy transfer between the Fenton-like reaction and necklace model microenvironment-modulated carbon dots. Under optimized experimental conditions, this proposed CL system exhibits a stable response to Co(II) over a concentration range from 1.0 to 1000nM with a detection limit as low as 0.67nM. Finally, this system has been successfully used for the detection of Co(II) in HepG2 cells, and the results were agreed with those obtained by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer–surfactant complexes
Fenton-like system
Chemiluminescence
Cobalt
Carbon dots
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of carbon dots
Principle of selective sensing for Co(II)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Selectivity of Co(II) sensing over some common metal ions utilizing CTAB@carbon dot-amplified Fenton-like CL reaction. The concentration of each metal ion was 0.5μM; (B) CL signals of the proposed system by adding different concentrations of Co(II). Inset: the calibration curve for Co(II). The experimental conditions were the same as those described in the legend of Fig. S3.

(A) The change of the CL intensity as a result of the injection of CTAB@carbon dot solution into Co(II)/Cu(II)–H2O2–OH− mixing solution at different time stages. Inset: schematic diagram of the bath method. Experimental procedures: 57μL 5mM H2O2+57μL 0.5mM Co(II)/Cu(II)+100μL 5.0mM NaOH+100μL CTAB@carbon dots; (B) ESR spectra of hydroxyl radicals generated from Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system and Cu(II)–H2O2–OH− system via their reaction with DMPO probe. Experimental procedure: 20μL 5.0mM H2O2+20μL 1.0μM Co(II)/Cu(II)+35μL 5.0mM NaOH+50μL 0.2M DMPO; (C) schematic illustration for the concentrating of hydroperoxyl anions in CTAB–PEG microenvironment.

(A) CL spectrum for CTAB@carbon dot-Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system; (B) Fluorescence spectra for CTAB@carbon dots before and after the CL reaction; (C) ESR spectra of nitroxide radicals generated via the reaction of TEMP probe for Co(II)–H2O2–OH− and CTAB@carbon dot-Co(II)–H2O2–OH− systems; (D) ESR spectra of nitroxide radicals generated via the reaction of TEMP probe for CTAB@carbon dots before and after the CL reaction. The experimental conditions of ESR spectra were the same as those described in the legend of Fig. 2B.
Possible CL mechanism for CTAB@carbon dot-Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system.
Determination of Co(II) in HepG2 cells (n=3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Sample 1 was exposed to 1.0μM CoCl2 for 24h; sample 2 was exposed to 0.1mM CoCl2 for 24h; sample 3 was exposed to 1.0mM CoCl2 for 24h and sample 4 was exposed to 5.0mM CoCl2 for 24h.

head.ce:title: High selectivity sensing of cobalt in HepG2 cells based on necklace model microenvironment-modulated carbon dot-improved chemiluminescence in Fenton-like system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Military Institute of Chinese Materia Medica, 302 Military Hospital of China, Beijing 100039, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, based on electronic spinning resonance and chemiluminescence (CL) measurements, it is found that the reaction kinetics of Co(II)-triggered Fenton-like reaction is much faster than those of other transition metal ions, which facilitates the observation of the strong CL signals from Co(II). Utilizing the polymer–surfactant complexes (i.e., necklace model) to act as a novel CL microenvironment, a highly selective CL sensing system for the detection of Co(II) has been successfully fabricated based on the CL resonance energy transfer between the Fenton-like reaction and necklace model microenvironment-modulated carbon dots. Under optimized experimental conditions, this proposed CL system exhibits a stable response to Co(II) over a concentration range from 1.0 to 1000nM with a detection limit as low as 0.67nM. Finally, this system has been successfully used for the detection of Co(II) in HepG2 cells, and the results were agreed with those obtained by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) method.

► The polymer–surfactant complexes are used for a novel CL microenvironment. ► Co(II)-triggered Fenton-like CL reaction can be discriminated from other transition metal ions. ► Necklace model microenvironment-modulated carbon dots act as CL resonance energy transfer acceptors. ► This proposed system has been successfully applied for sensing Co(II) in HepG2 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt is an essential trace metal nutrient for life, but exposure to excessive amounts of cobalt is associated with various diseases and disabilities such as asthma, decreased cardiac output, cardiac enlargements, heart disease, lung disease, dermatitis and vasodilation (Barras and Fontecave, 2011; Cakar et al., 2009; Zeno et al., 2009). Therefore, monitoring of trace amounts of cobalt in biological and environmental samples offers potentially useful tools to help elucidate its physiological and pathological effects. Overestimation and underestimation of Co(II) content have a great effect in influencing disease diagnosis and therapeutic drug monitoring. Accordingly, the specific and rapid sensing for Co(II) is vital in clinical analysis. Unfortunately, there are only a few reports on the highly selective detection for Co(II) without separation techniques (Au-Yeung et al., 2012; Lin et al., 2008; Maity and Govindaraju, 2011). Currently, the chemiluminescence (CL) technique has been employed to detect Co(II) by virtue of its high sensitivity, high-speed analysis and non-destructiveness (Economou et al., 2001; Lok et al., 2012; Sung et al., 2011). In spite of these advantages, CL faces a challenge of poor selectivity toward Co(II) due to serious mutual interference from other metal ions. Therefore, many researchers have made great efforts to improve the CL selectivity toward Co(II) by sample pretreatments or separation techniques. To the best of our knowledge, specific and readily deployable tools for reliable and precise CL measurements of Co(II) are not available at this time. Accordingly, it is still an active field as well as a great challenge to develop a new and selective CL method for Co(II).

The decompositions of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) catalyzed by transition metal ions, such as Co(II), Mn(II), Fe(III) and Cu(II) in homogeneous and heterogeneous systems are known as Fenton-like reactions or modified Fenton processes (Charkoudian et al., 2006). Under acidic conditions, the generally accepted mechanism identifies hydroxyl radical (OH) as the active oxidizing intermediate in the Fenton-like reactions. However, the production of OH radical is significantly reduced in basic solutions (Chen et al., 2011). Therefore, there is no conclusive answer for or against the involvement of OH radical from H2O2 in basic solutions (Ou et al., 2002). Moreover, transition metal ion-catalyzed H2O2 decomposition in basic solutions can emit an ultra-weak CL as a result of the generation of the excited singlet oxygen dimol species (O2)2 * and a light enhancer (e.g., luminol, amino acid, DNA and gold nanoparticles) was employed for amplifying the light intensity (Kladna et al., 2003; Li et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2005). After an extensive literature search, it is confirmed that Co(II) and Cu(II) can exhibit excellent cataluminescence characteristics and a higher luminescence efficiency is obtained from Co(II) in basic solutions; however, a typical Fe(II) in Fenton's reagent cannot catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to generate the CL in basic solutions (Zhang et al., 2012). These interesting phenomena, along with their potential applications are worthwhile for further investigation.

Fluorescent carbon dots constitute a fascinating class of recently discovered nanocarbons with sizes below 10nm. Typically, carbon dots contain many carboxylic acid moieties at their surface, thus imparting them with excellent water solubility and facile functionalization (Baker and Baker, 2010; Bourlinos et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2006). Owing to their stable photoluminescence, low toxicity, easy preparation and excellent biocompatibility, these strongly emissive carbon dots have drawn increasing applications in biomedical imaging and optoelectronic devices, which are similar to or beyond those of the widely pursued toxic metal-based quantum dot counterparts (Cao et al., 2007; Dong et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2009). Recently, it was reported that classical oxidants (e.g., cerium (IV) and potassium permanganate) and reactive oxygen species (e.g., peroxynitrous acid and peroxymonosulfite) can trigger the CL emission of fluorescent carbon dots by electron-transfer annihilation or resonance energy transfers (Lin et al., 2011, 2012; Xue et al., 2011). These interesting CL properties may offer new opportunities in potentially using carbon dots for analytical applications, in addition to their being valuable to the effort on mechanistic elucidation.

The polymer–surfactant systems are the combinations of a certain polymer with a specific surfactant at specific ratios that control the micro heterogeneous environmental properties of the bulk aqueous solution and at the solution interface (Mace et al., 2012; Sahoo et al., 2009). The interaction of polymers with surfactants in aqueous solution as well as at the interfaces is an interesting subject. Recently, polymer–surfactant interactions are extensively investigated by fluorescence (Hoffmann et al., 2012; Panmai et al., 2002; Sukul et al., 2000), light scattering (Ruchmann et al., 2011), and photon correlation spectroscopy (Cattoz et al., 2012). The surfactant-polymer mixture is more advantageous than the polymer or surfactants alone because of their complementary or synergistic roles. The significant findings in terms of fluorescence studies indicate a long fluorescence lifetime with high emission efficiency in surfactant–polymer aggregates. As a result, we can make predictions that the CL behaviors in surfactant–polymer microenvironments are very different from those in micelles. However, there have been no reports on the influences of surfactant–polymer microenvironments on the CL emission.

In the present work, a facile microwave pyrolysis approach to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots is carried out by combining PEG 1500 with serine in the presence of glycerin as a co-reactant. The PEG-passivated carbon dots were modulated with cationic cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) surfactant to form the CTAB–PEG microenvironment (denoted as CTAB@carbon dots). Herein, employing CTAB@carbon dots as new CL amplification agents, we investigated the CL intensity of the Fenton-like systems of various metal ions. Interestingly, Co(II) among all the metal ions tested can generate the strongest CL intensity, which can be explained by examining the reaction kinetics and electronic spinning resonance (ESR) measurements of different metal ions. It is noted that the reaction kinetics of Co(II) is fast, whereas those of other metal ions, such as Cu(II), Mn(II), and Fe(II) are relatively slow. This work is of importance for gaining a thorough understanding of the Fenton-like reaction essence in basic conditions. In particular, we demonstrated that it is possible to apply the CTAB@carbon dot-amplified Fenton-like CL system for the detection of Co(II) in HepG2 cells with high selectivity and sensitivity. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first example using a polymer–surfactant complex as a novel CL microenvironment.

All chemicals were of analytical grade without further purification. CoCl2·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, FeCl3, CuCl2·2H2O, MnSO4·H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CrCl3·6H2O, AlCl3·6H2O, ZnCl2, glycerin, NaOH, and Triton X-100 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Thiourea and NaN3 were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory (Tianjin, China). Ascorbic acid was purchased from Beijing Aoboxing Bio-tech Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-4-piperidine (TEMP), CTAB, tetradecyltrimethylammonium bromide (TTAB), dodecyltrimethylammonium bromide (DTAB), 1,4-diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane (DABCO), polyethylene glycol 1500 (PEG 1500), serine and sodium dodecyl sulfonate (SDS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) was purchased from Nacalai Tesque Inc. (Tokyo, Japan). 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was obtained from Tokyo Kasei Kogyo Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Working solutions of H2O2 were prepared daily from 30% (v/v) H2O2 (Beijing Chemical Reagent Company, China). A 0.1M CoCl2 stock solution was prepared by dissolving CoCl2 with deionized water (18.2MΩcm, Milli Q, Millipore, Barnstead, CA, USA). The working solutions of CoCl2 were freshly prepared by diluting the stock solution with deionized water.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) photographs were taken on a Tecnai G220 TEM from USA FEI Company. ESR measurements were performed on a JES-FA200 spectrometer (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The CL spectrum was obtained using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) at a slit of 20.0nm and a scanning rate of 1200nm/min. UV–vis spectra were measured on a USB 4000 miniature fiber optic spectrometer in absorbance mode with a DH-2000 deuterium and tungsten halogen light source (Ocean Optics, Dunedin, FL). The fluorescence spectra were performed using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. A 800W microwave oven (G8023CSL-K3, Galanz, China) was used for the preparation of carbon dots. The CL detection was conducted on a BPCL luminescence analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China). An Agilent 7700 ICP-MS system (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA) was used to detect the Co(II) of HepG2 cells samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were prepared via a previously reported microwave pyrolysis approach (Lin et al., 2011). Briefly, 1.0g PEG 1500 was combined with 0.5g serine in the presence of 15mL glycerin to form a transparent solution, followed by heating in a 800W microwave oven for 7min. The solution changed from colorless to dark brown over the time course of the reaction (Fig. S1). Fig. S2 showed the characterization of the as-prepared carbon dots, including the UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescent spectra and TEM image. The as-prepared carbon dots were purified by the dialysis membrane with a molecular weight of cutoff 2000. The obtained carbon dots remain stable for several months in a refrigerator at 4°C.

The schematic diagram of the flow injection analysis (FIA) was illustrated in Fig. S3. It consisted of two peristaltic pumps (BT-100M, Baoding, China), a 80μL loop injector, a six-way injection valve (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan), a quartz tube, and a BPCL luminescence analyzer. The mixed solution of CTAB@carbon dots and Co(II) standard or sample solution was injected into the carrier stream (H2O2, 3.5mL/min) through a valve injector with a 80μL sample loop, and mixed with NaOH (2.8mL/min) through a three-way connector. The signal output from the CL reaction was detected by the PMT. The signals were imported to the computer for data acquisition.

Human hepatocellular liver carcinoma cells (HepG2) were provided by the Institute of Basic Medical Sciences Chinese Academy of Medical. HepG2 cells were cultured in high glucose Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, Gibco, NY, USA) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotics (penicillin/streptomycin, 100U/mL). The cells were kept in a 37°C, 5% CO2 humidified incubator, and then were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1.0×105 cells/mL. Once the dishes became confluent by 70–80%, a concentration of CoCl2 prepared in DMEM was added and incubated for 24h. After being washed three times with phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.2), the cultured HepG2 cells were detached by digestion with trypsin for 1min at room temperature. The cells were collected in a tube and centrifuged at 800rpm for 5min. The treatment for the control group HepG2 cells was the same as described above, except that no Co(II) standard solution was added to the medium. To determine Co(II), the precipitated HepG2 cells were digested by using 100μL 1.0M HNO3 at 80°C in an ultrasonic bath for 12h until the solution was clear. Finally the samples were diluted to 5.0mL. The pH of the samples was adjusted to pH 7.0 with 5.0M NaOH, and then the as-prepared samples were analyzed immediately by the proposed CL method or the ICP-MS method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of selective sensing for Co(II)
ce:section.ce:para: In a flow-injection CL setup, we investigated the CL intensity of the CTAB@carbon dot-amplified Fenton-like reaction of various metal ions. As shown in Fig. 1A, upon the addition of 0.5μM Co(II), an observable increase in the CL intensity was noted. However, little CL change occurred in the presence of other metal ions, even at higher concentrations. The highly selective response of the present CL system toward Co(II) is striking. This can be explained by the batch method (inset of Fig. 2A). Two curves recorded the change of the CL intensity after the CTAB@carbon dot solution was injected into 0.5mM Co(II)–H2O2–OH− or Cu(II)–H2O2–OH− mixing solution at different time stages (0–60s). We can observe from Fig. 2A that the CL intensity of the CTAB@carbon dot-Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system was much stronger than that of the CTAB@carbon dot-Cu(II)–H2O2–OH− system at the beginning (i.e., 0s). However, the CL intensity of the CTAB@carbon dot-Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system decreased by almost 95% from 0 to 20s. In contrast, the CL intensity of the CTAB@carbon dot-Cu(II)–H2O2–OH− system decreased by almost 78% from 0 to 20s, and remained constant until 60s. Accordingly, in the flow-injection system, the higher flow rate should be used to make a big CL difference between Co(II) and other metal ions. On the other hand, as a specific target molecule of OH radical, DMPO was used to detect the amount of OH radicals available during Fenton-like reactions (about 45s). The results showed that the intensity of ESR from Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system was larger than that from Cu(II)–H2O2–OH− system (Fig. 2B). Note that ESR signals were acquired as soon as possible after the Co(II)/Cu(II)–H2O2–OH− system was mixed with DMPO. These results demonstrated that the rapid production of OH radicals in abundant amounts from Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system may be responsible for the highly selective response of the present CL system toward Co(II).

Several theoretical research groups have proposed models to explain the polymer–surfactant interactions, called “necklace model” (Hoffmann et al., 2012; Sukul et al., 2000). In this process, the polymer–surfactant aggregates consist of a series of spherical micelles surrounded by the polymer segments and connected by the polymer strands. In this work, we examined the effects of different surfactants on the proposed CL system including cationic, anionic and nonionic surfactants. As shown in Fig. S4 and S5, the addition of three cationic surfactants (i.e., TTAB, DTAB and CTAB) can enhance the CL intensity, and the CL intensity increased with the increasing of the concentration of each cationic surfactant. Note that CTAB was the most promising one in three cationic surfactants examined because of the low critical micelle concentration (CMC) of CTAB (Lu et al., 2002). However, the anionic SDS and nonionic Triton X-100 did not show a significant influence on the CL intensity. A previous contrast experiment has demonstrated that a weak CL signal was observed when only CTAB was injected into the CL system, due to the fact that the local microenvironment in micelle media is significantly different from the homogeneous media (Fig. S5) (Correa et al., 2012). From the “necklace model” of polymer–surfactant interactions, we can easily predict that with increasing CTAB concentrations, more hydrophobic strands of the polymer molecules (PEG) are connected with spherical CTAB micelles. At high concentrations of CTAB, PEG molecules become saturated with micelles, and thus the CL intensity becomes almost flat. Similarly, anionic SDS and nonionic Triton X-100 can form “necklace model” with PEG. However, the hydroperoxyl anions (HO2 −) produced from the dissociation of H2O2 in alkaline environment cannot bind onto the surface of anionic or nonionic micelles via the electrostatic attraction, which occurs easily for cationic micelles (Fig. 2C) (Asker et al., 2009). Moreover, we investigated the geometrical configuration on the surface of PEG-passivated carbon dots by comparing zeta potential before and after addition of CTAB into carbon dot solution. The zeta potential of PEG-passivated carbon dots was negatively charged (−2.5mV), indicating a strong attachment of PEG at the surface of carbon dots (Baker and Baker, 2010). Interestingly, the zeta potential gradually increased in magnitude upon adding CTAB in the range of 0.25–5.0mM. These results demonstrated that the interactions of PEG with CTAB can easily form the “necklace model” microenvironment even at lower CTAB concentrations, known as the critical association concentration. Note that the critical micelle concentration of CTAB is 0.94mM, which is often much higher than critical association concentration (Sukul et al., 2000).

It was concluded that the CL amplification of the PEG-passivated carbon dots was supposed to originate from the processes of electron-transfer annihilation or resonance energy transfers (Lin et al., 2011, 2012; Xue et al., 2011). In this case, in order to confirm the emitting species of CTAB@carbon dot-Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system, the following experiments were carried out.

Table S1 showed the effects of different active radical scavengers on the CL intensity of the present system. As well-known scavengers for singlet oxygen (1O2), both 1.0mM DABCO or 1.0mM NaN3 did not quench the CL intensity, which provided strong evidence that 1O2 did not contribute to the observed CL (Kazakov et al., 2007). However, the CL intensity was greatly quenched by 0.1mM ascorbic acid or 50mM thiourea (scavengers of OH radicals), and the quenching effect increased with the increase of the scavengers. It implied that abundant OH radicals were released in the reaction (Sikora et al., 2011). Moreover, NBT reduction is often used as an indicator of superoxide radical anion (O2 −). When 10mM NBT was added to the present CL system, an obvious color change from yellow to blue occurred, along with a remarkable decrease in CL intensity, indicating the existence of O2 − radical (Yamakoshi et al., 2003).

The CL spectrum for CTAB@carbon dot-Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system was acquired using a FL-7000 model spectrofluorimeter combined with a flow-injection system. It could be clearly seen from Fig. 3A that there was only one emission band in the range of 400–650nm with a maximum emission peak at 535nm. The fluorescence spectra were employed to characterize CTAB@carbon dots before and after the CL reaction. It was found that the maximum emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity of CTAB@carbon dots remained unchanged in the presence of Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system (Fig. 3B), indicating that the nanocrystal lattice structure of carbon dots was not destroyed after the CL reaction. Thus, the CL enhancement of CTAB@carbon dots was ascribed to resonance energy transfer process rather than electron-transfer annihilation process. However, the maximum CL emission wavelength (535nm) was red-shifted from the fluorescence maximum (420nm) fluorescence spectrum. This red shift between the fluorescence and CL emission was also observed in the previous experiment (Lin et al., 2011). The accepted explain was as follows: fluorescence emission mainly occurs through excitation and emission within the quantum dot core; however, CL emission depends more sensitively on surface states than fluorescence emission. Quantum dot cores have band gaps greater than the energy separation of surface states. Therefore, the difference between fluorescence emission and CL results from a major contribution of the surface states in CL emission (Ding et al., 2002; Myung et al., 2002; Zheng et al., 2009).

The possibility of generating 1O2 in the proposed CL system was detected by means of the room-temperature ESR method. As a specific spin-trapping reagent for 1O2, TEMP can react with 1O2 to produce a stable nitroxide adduct (2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidine-N-oxide, TEMPO) (Xue et al., 2011). Fig. 3C showed that 1O2 could not be generated by Co(II)–H2O2–OH− and CTAB@carbon dot-Co(II)–H2O2–OH− systems, which were in agreement with the results obtained by active radical scavengers (Table S1). In addition, the ground-state properties of luminescent species in the carbon dots were also investigated by ESR measurement. CTAB@carbon dots had an ESR signal at g=1.9991 (Fig. 3D). However, the g-value of CTAB@carbon dots revealed little shift when CTAB@carbon dots reacted with Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system, indicating that CTAB@carbon dots acted as energy acceptors during a CL resonance energy transfer (Lin et al., 2011).

The mechanism of the present CL system was summarized in Fig. 4. H2O2 can dissociate into HO2 − anions at elevated pH (McMurray and Wilson, 1999; Zhang et al., 2005). The necklace structure obtained by PEG–CTAB interaction can easily concentrate HO2 − anions via the electrostatic attraction. The CTAB hydrophobic microenvironment accommodated the output of OH radical by the reaction of Co(II) with H2O2. OH radical could initiate a sequence of reactions in alkaline solution to yield O2 − radical (Li et al., 2011; Yamakoshi et al., 2003). Recombination of O2 − radical may produce the energy-rich excited singlet oxygen dimol species (O2)2 ⁎ (Chen et al., 2012). The excited molecules (O2)2 ⁎ can transfer their energy to highly luminescent carbon dots. The excited state of carbon dots resulted in a strong emission when it returned to the ground state (Liu et al., 2010).

Under the optimum conditions (Fig. S6), we examined the analytical performances of the proposed CL method for sensing Co(II) by a flow injecting procedure (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. 1B, the calibration curve was found to be linear from 1.0 to 1000nM for Co(II) with a regression line y=0.1059x−1.1849 (R 2=0.9920), where y is the relative CL intensity and x is the concentration of Co(II). The relative standard deviation for eleven repeated measurement of 10nM Co(II) was 1.0%. Note that the linear ranges for Co(II) reached three orders of magnitude, and the detection limit for Co(II) (S/N=3) was 0.67nM, which were about 10–100 times lower than those of the previous results obtained by well-developed analytical methods (Au-Yeung et al., 2012; Lin et al., 2008; Maity and Govindaraju, 2011). Furthermore, the present CL detection method was compared with other metal ions sensors (Hao et al., 2012; Ma et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2011). It was found our proposed method showed low detection limit, wide linear dynamic range and simple procedure. These results showed that the proposed CL system had very promising application in the detection of Co(II). The applicability and reliability of the proposed methodology was further evaluated by detecting Co(II) in HepG2 cells. The calibration curve was used for the determination of Co(II) in HepG2 cells. The results of the proposed method were in agreement with those obtained by the standard ICP-MS method ( Table 1). The satisfactory results indicated that the proposed method was highly selective and sensitive toward Co(II) and it is promising for the determination of Co(II) in clinical analysis.

In summary, using the “necklace model” of polymer–surfactant interaction on the surface of carbon dots as a novel CL microenvironment, we have designed a highly selective and sensitive CL probe for Co(II). The CL reaction kinetics and ESR studies of different transition metal ion-triggered Fenton-like reaction demonstrated that the rapid production of OH radicals in abundant amounts from Co(II)–H2O2–OH− system may be responsible for the highly selective response of the present CL system toward Co(II). Most importantly, the novel probe has solved the problem of serious interference from other transition metal ions generally encountered by previously reported typical CL probes for Co(II) with luminol-H2O2 system. The proposed CL sensing system has been demonstrated to determine Co(II) in HepG2 cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21077008), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-11–0561), and the 973 Program (2011CBA00503).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2013.01.056.
